6WlU86IAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Jon Duke is a researcher based at **Georgia Tech**, working at the intersection of **clinical informatics**, **data science**, and *computational methods for healthcare*. He is an active contributor to the international Observational Health Data Sciences and Informatics (**OHDSI**) community and collaborates extensively with academic medical centers and research networks. His work centers on developing methods and tools that enable scalable, reproducible observational research from routine clinical data, with particular emphasis on <u>electronic health records (EHR) interoperability and transformation</u>, <u>privacy-preserving synthetic data</u>, and <u>explainable clinical prediction</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Duke’s publications span methodological and applied topics in health data science. He has helped articulate and operationalize the OHDSI vision for large-scale observational research (“Observational Health Data Sciences and Informatics (OHDSI): opportunities for observational researchers”, 2015), worked on transformation and quality assurance of real-world data into a common data model (e.g., OMOP/ CDM work on CDM transformation and data-quality tooling such as Achilles Heel), and contributed to large-scale comparative effectiveness and safety studies of antihypertensive and other drug classes. On the methodological side he has advanced *synthetic EHR generation* using deep generative models (GANs and conditional VAEs; “Generating multi-label discrete patient records using generative adversarial networks”, 2017; “EVA: Generating longitudinal electronic health records using conditional variational autoencoders”, 2021), and developed approaches for *explainable clinical NLP* to predict medical codes from clinical text (“Explainable prediction of medical codes from clinical text”, 2018). He has also published on clinical decision support and human-centered visualization for ICU and medication-review tasks, drug–drug interaction discovery and evaluation, and the integration of interoperability standards (e.g., FHIR) into public health reporting pipelines.

---

## Notable Works
- “Observational Health Data Sciences and Informatics (OHDSI): opportunities for observational researchers” (2015) — a foundational, highly cited piece that helped define a global collaborative ecosystem and set priorities for large-scale, reproducible observational analyses across standardized clinical data sources.  
- “Generating multi-label discrete patient records using generative adversarial networks” (2017) — introduced and validated GAN-based approaches for producing synthetic EHRs to enable data sharing while mitigating privacy risk, influencing subsequent work on privacy-preserving clinical data generation.  
- “Explainable prediction of medical codes from clinical text” (2018) — advanced methods for mapping free-text clinical notes to structured medical codes with interpretability, addressing both automation of coding and the need for transparent model outputs in clinical settings.

---

## Academic Background
Jon Duke is affiliated with **Georgia Tech** and is a prominent collaborator within large multi-institution consortia (notably OHDSI) and clinical informatics centers (frequent co-authorship with groups such as Regenstrief and other academic medical centers). His publication record demonstrates a sustained focus on translational informatics problems that bring together epidemiology, computer science, and clinical practice. With a total citation count on the order of **6,487**, his work is widely cited and has influenced standards and tools for observational research, synthetic data generation, and clinical decision support. Duke’s career emphasizes interdisciplinary partnership, community-oriented open-source tool development, and methodological rigor in deploying data-driven solutions for real-world healthcare problems.

1vwuDM4AAAAJ.keywords: Cryptography, security, secure computation, zero knowledge proofs, garbled circuits, oblivious transfer extension, oblivious PRF, private set intersection, privacy-preserving databases, non-interactive zero-knowledge
1vwuDM4AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Vladimir Kolesnikov is a faculty researcher in the College of Computing at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group focused on *applied cryptography and privacy-preserving systems*. His work centers on practical techniques for secure computation and cryptographic protocols, with particular emphasis on <u>garbled circuits</u>, <u>secure multi-party computation (MPC)</u>, and <u>private set operations</u>. Kolesnikov's research blends theoretical primitives with systems-level implementation, producing both foundational constructions (e.g., optimizations for garbled-circuit evaluation) and scalable systems (e.g., private DBMS and smart-grid protocols) that make *privacy-preserving computation* feasible in practice.

---

## Research Areas
Kolesnikov's research spans the design, analysis, and engineering of cryptographic protocols that enable computation on private data without disclosure. He is a leading contributor to the body of work on <u>garbled-circuit optimizations</u> (introducing and improving techniques such as Free-XOR and later fleXOR), and has developed multiple building blocks that reduce the cost of secure two-party and multi-party protocols—covering gates and arithmetic operations, cut-and-choose strategies, and amortization techniques for repeated executions. His papers also treat efficient primitives that underpin large-scale applications: optimized OT extension and oblivious PRFs for lightweight cryptographic transfers, new paradigms for *private set intersection (PSI)* and *private set union (PSU)* using symmetric-key techniques, and non-interactive zero-knowledge proofs via the MPC-in-the-head paradigm for post-quantum signatures. Complementing these cryptographic advances, Kolesnikov has produced systems-oriented work on scalable private DBMS (Blind Seer), secure evaluation of branching programs with medical applications, and secure protocols tailored to cyber-physical settings such as the smart grid.

---

## Notable Works
- "Improved garbled circuit: Free XOR gates and applications" (2008) — Introduced the *Free-XOR* technique that makes XOR gates essentially cost-free in garbled-circuit protocols, significantly improving practical two-party secure function evaluation and influencing a generation of 2PC implementations.  
- "A pragmatic introduction to secure multi-party computation" (2018) — A widely-cited, accessible survey and roadmap that synthesizes theory and practice in MPC, clarifying design choices and bridging the gap between cryptographic research and applied system development.  
- "Efficient batched oblivious PRF with applications to private set intersection" (2016) — Developed lightweight, batched OPRF protocols that enable practical, high-throughput PSI constructions using symmetric-key techniques, reducing reliance on expensive public-key operations for many real-world private set tasks.

---

## Academic Background
Kolesnikov has been an active researcher in cryptography and secure computation since the early 2000s, authoring influential papers across theory and systems and accruing over 8,700 citations to date. His publication record includes foundational conference and journal contributions as well as patents and collaborative projects with industry research labs (e.g., work tied to Bell Labs / Alcatel-Lucent and other applied research programs). His portfolio demonstrates sustained impact on both the theoretical underpinnings (garbled circuits, OT extension, zero-knowledge) and practical deployments (scalable private DBMS, smart-grid security, and privacy-preserving biometric search). He maintains a lab page and resources for students and collaborators at his homepage (https://www.cc.gatech.edu/~vlad/), and is widely recognized for mentoring graduate students and driving cross-disciplinary work that brings cryptographic techniques into operational systems.
